gate_type: capability check manage_options + WordPress options.php nonce (settings_fields)
reasoning: The exploitation chain requires two gate layers that are both admin-only. First, the settings form in inc/options.php posts to WordPress core options.php and calls settings_fields('header-and-footer-scripts'), which emits a wp_nonce field tied to the 'header-and-footer-scripts' options group. WordPress options.php enforces manage_options capability AND verifies this nonce before processing any registered settings; unauthenticated or low-privilege users are rejected before update_option() is ever called. Second, even if options.php were bypassed, the update_option_jamify_hfs_allow_author and update_option_jamify_hfs_allow_contributor hooks in the constructor fire only as a consequence of update_option() succeeding, which itself requires the caller to have already passed the options.php gate. There is no alternative code path in the plugin that calls update_option for these two keys without administrator authentication. No REST endpoint, AJAX handler, or public-facing route in the plugin touches these option keys. The nonce is generated via wp_create_nonce() inside settings_fields(), which is only rendered on the admin options page that itself requires manage_options to access; there is no place in the plugin or WordPress core where this nonce is exposed to unauthenticated users. The finding is a genuine logic/design flaw (scope-exceeding unfiltered_html grant, especially impactful on multisite), but the trigger is fully gated behind administrator credentials. On multisite the impact is elevated because a site admin (non-super-admin, who has manage_options on their subsite) can use this to grant unfiltered_html to Authors/Contributors, bypassing the multisite design that intentionally withholds unfiltered_html from non-super-admins — but even this path requires the site admin to be authenticated. No unauthenticated or subscriber/contributor-level attacker can reach the vulnerable code without first obtaining administrator (or site-admin on multisite) credentials.
